schools.summary: The new heaven and new earth: 'and I saw a new heaven and a new earth: for the first heaven and the first earth were passed away; and there was no more sea' — Barnes holds the 'first heaven and first earth passed away' not by annihilation but by renovation (the present world purified and changed for the better by the conflagration, 2 Peter 3:10-13, not that the heaven and earth were annihilated but that they are made new); 'a new heaven and a new earth' (Isaiah 65:17; 66:22; 2 Peter 3:13) the renewed, glorified creation, the final abode of the righteous (the new order succeeding the old, fitted for the redeemed); 'there was no more sea' (the sea, the symbol of instability, separation, and the restless nations, gone, whether literal or symbolic, its absence marking the perfect peace and unity of the new creation, no more division or unrest) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

Victorinus: the first world passes away 'by the conflagration of the supernal fire,' the heaven and earth changed for the better (the incorruption and immortality given them), not annihilated — the renovation and glorification of the present creation rather than its destruction and replacement; the saints, their hearts purified, dwell in the renewed creation. Bede reads the new heaven and earth as the glorified state of the redeemed succeeding the old. The mode of the renewal (purification vs new creation) is the crux.

debates.question: Are the new heaven and new earth the renovation of the present cosmos or a wholly new creation?

sides.position: Renovation/purification — the present heaven and earth changed for the better by fire, not annihilated
The destruction of the old creation and the making of a wholly new one

themes: I saw a new heaven and a new earth: for the first heaven and the first earth were passed away; and there was no more sea
the new heaven and new earth
the crux of renewal or new creation
the final, glorious abode of the righteous

schools.summary: The New Jerusalem as bride: 'and I John saw the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down from God out of heaven, prepared as a bride adorned for her husband' — John seeing the holy city, the new Jerusalem, descending from God out of heaven (the city the dwelling of God with the redeemed, v.3, the glorified church/community of the saved, coming down from God, of heavenly origin and gift); 'the holy city, new Jerusalem' (3:12; Galatians 4:26; Hebrews 12:22) the figure of the redeemed people of God, the glorified church, in their final, blessed state (the new Jerusalem in contrast to fallen Babylon, ch. 17-18, the holy city of God's dwelling); 'prepared as a bride adorned for her husband' (the new Jerusalem the bride of the Lamb, v.9; 19:7, adorned and ready for her husband, Christ, the church as the holy city and the bride one, the figure of the redeemed prepared in beauty for union with Christ) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down from God out of heaven
prepared as a bride adorned for her husband
the New Jerusalem descending as a bride
the glorified church, the dwelling of God, as the Lamb's bride

schools.summary: God dwelling with his people: 'and I heard a great voice out of heaven saying, Behold, the tabernacle of God is with men, and he will dwell with them, and they shall be his people, and God himself shall be with them, and be their God' — a great voice announcing that God's tabernacle (dwelling) is now with men, God himself dwelling among the redeemed (the consummation of the covenant promise — God with his people — the fulfillment of the tabernacle and temple, John 1:14; Ezekiel 37:27); 'they shall be his people, and God himself shall be with them, and be their God' the covenant formula (Leviticus 26:11-12; Jeremiah 31:33) consummated (God dwelling with his people, they his, he their God, the perfect, eternal communion of God and the redeemed); the essence of the new creation's joy God's immediate presence (the tabernacle of God with men the heart of the blessed state, the redeemed enjoying unbroken fellowship with God) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: Behold, the tabernacle of God is with men, and he will dwell with them
they shall be his people, and God himself shall be with them, and be their God
God dwelling with his people in the new creation
the consummated covenant, the perfect communion of God and the redeemed

schools.summary: The end of death and sorrow: 'and God shall wipe away all tears from their eyes; and there shall be no more death, neither sorrow, nor crying, neither shall there be any more pain: for the former things are passed away' — God himself wiping away every tear from the eyes of the redeemed (7:17; Isaiah 25:8, the figure of perfect comfort and the end of all sorrow, God's own hand removing their grief); 'no more death, neither sorrow, nor crying, neither... any more pain' (death, sorrow, crying, and pain — all the evils and miseries of the old order — abolished in the new creation, the redeemed forever free from suffering and death); 'for the former things are passed away' (the old order, with all its evils, having passed, the former things — the world of sin, death, and sorrow — gone, the new creation wholly free of them, the basis of the abolition of suffering the passing of the old world) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: God shall wipe away all tears from their eyes; and there shall be no more death, neither sorrow, nor crying, neither shall there be any more pain
for the former things are passed away
the end of death, sorrow, and pain in the new creation
the perfect, eternal freedom from all suffering

schools.summary: God makes all things new: 'and he that sat upon the throne said, Behold, I make all things new. And he said unto me, Write: for these words are true and faithful' — God himself, from the throne, declaring that he makes all things new (the renewal of all creation, cf. 2 Corinthians 5:17; Isaiah 43:19, the comprehensive renovation/recreation of all things in the new order, God the author of the new creation); 'I make all things new' (the whole order renewed, not merely new things but all things made new, the transformation of the entire creation into the glorified state, God's renewing work embracing all); 'Write: for these words are true and faithful' (God bidding John write, affirming that these words are true and faithful — trustworthy, genuine — the promises of the new creation sure and certain, God's word guaranteeing their fulfillment, the reader assured of the truth of these things) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: he that sat upon the throne said, Behold, I make all things new
Write: for these words are true and faithful
God's declaration that he makes all things new
the comprehensive renovation of all creation, the promise sure

schools.summary: Alpha and Omega and the water of life: 'and he said unto me, It is done. I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end. I will give unto him that is athirst of the fountain of the water of life freely' — God declaring 'It is done' (the work of the new creation completed, as 16:17) and proclaiming himself 'Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end' (1:8; 22:13, the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet, affirming God's eternity and his sovereignty over all history, the origin and goal of all, the One who completes what he began); 'I will give unto him that is athirst of the fountain of the water of life freely' (to the thirsty, God giving freely of the fountain of the water of life, 22:17; John 4:14; Isaiah 55:1, the free gift of eternal life and the satisfaction of the soul's thirst, the gracious invitation amid the consummation) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: It is done. I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end
I will give unto him that is athirst of the fountain of the water of life freely
God as Alpha and Omega and the water of life
the eternal God who completes all and gives eternal life freely

schools.summary: The overcomer's inheritance: 'he that overcometh shall inherit all things; and I will be his God, and he shall be my son' — the one who overcomes (the victorious believer, as in the seven letters) shall inherit all things, and God will be his God and he God's son (the consummate reward — the inheritance of all the blessings of the new creation, and the relation of sonship to God); 'shall inherit all things' (heir of all the blessings of the new creation, the holy city, and the presence of God, the comprehensive inheritance of the redeemed, cf. Romans 8:17; Matthew 5:5); 'I will be his God, and he shall be my son' (the covenant relation, 2 Samuel 7:14; cf. the covenant formula, v.3, applied to the overcomer, God his God and he God's son, the intimate, filial relation of the redeemed to God, the adoption consummated) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: He that overcometh shall inherit all things; and I will be his God, and he shall be my son
the overcomer's inheritance and sonship
the consummate inheritance of the new creation
the filial relation of the redeemed to God

schools.summary: The catalogue of the lost: 'but the fearful, and unbelieving, and the abominable, and murderers, and whoremongers, and sorcerers, and idolaters, and all liars, shall have their part in the lake which burneth with fire and brimstone: which is the second death' — over against the overcomer who inherits all (v.7), this catalogue of sinners (the cowardly/fearful, unbelieving, abominable, murderers, fornicators, sorcerers, idolaters, liars) excluded (the list of the unredeemed who have no part in the new creation); 'the fearful (cowardly), and unbelieving' heading the list (those who, through cowardice or unbelief, fall away or refuse Christ, their fear of man and lack of faith condemning them, the catalogue then naming the grosser sins); 'shall have their part in the lake which burneth with fire and brimstone: which is the second death' (the lost assigned to the lake of fire, the second death, 20:14, their portion eternal perdition, the solemn contrast to the overcomer's inheritance, exclusion from the new creation and consignment to the second death) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the fearful, and unbelieving, and the abominable, and murderers, and whoremongers, and sorcerers, and idolaters, and all liars
shall have their part in the lake which burneth with fire and brimstone: which is the second death
the catalogue of the lost in the second death
the unredeemed excluded from the new creation

schools.summary: The angel shows the bride: 'and there came unto me one of the seven angels which had the seven vials full of the seven last plagues, and talked with me, saying, Come hither, I will shew thee the bride, the Lamb's wife' — one of the vial-angels (as the one who showed the harlot, 17:1) now inviting John to see 'the bride, the Lamb's wife' (the deliberate contrast — the same kind of angel shows the harlot Babylon and the bride the New Jerusalem); 'the bride, the Lamb's wife' the New Jerusalem (v.2, 10), the glorified church, the bride of the Lamb (19:7), the holy city and the bride one — the redeemed people of God in their final, glorious union with Christ; the angel who showed the judgment of the great whore (17:1) now showing the bride (the New Jerusalem, the faithful bride, set over against Babylon, the unfaithful harlot, the deliberate contrast crowning the book — the doom of the harlot and the glory of the bride) — Barnes, Robertson, Henry.

themes: Come hither, I will shew thee the bride, the Lamb's wife
the angel offering to show the bride, the Lamb's wife
the New Jerusalem, the glorified church, set over against Babylon
the bride and the harlot contrasted

schools.summary: The holy city shown: 'and he carried me away in the spirit to a great and high mountain, and shewed me that great city, the holy Jerusalem, descending out of heaven from God' — John carried in the Spirit to a great, high mountain (the vantage for viewing the city, as Ezekiel 40:2) and shown the holy Jerusalem descending from God (the glorified city/church displayed in its descent); 'a great and high mountain' the elevated vantage from which the whole city may be seen (the parallel to Ezekiel's vision of the city, Ezekiel 40:2, the place of revelation); 'that great city, the holy Jerusalem, descending out of heaven from God' (the holy city, v.2, of heavenly origin, coming down from God, the glorified church, the bride, displayed in her descent, the New Jerusalem God's gift, from heaven, not of earthly making) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: he carried me away in the spirit to a great and high mountain, and shewed me that great city, the holy Jerusalem, descending out of heaven from God
John shown the holy city from a high mountain
the glorified city of heavenly origin displayed in her descent
the New Jerusalem God's gift from heaven

schools.summary: The city's glory: 'having the glory of God: and her light was like unto a stone most precious, even like a jasper stone, clear as crystal' — the holy city having the glory of God (the divine presence, his shekinah, illumining her), her light (radiance) like a most precious stone, a jasper, clear as crystal (the city shining with the very glory of God); 'having the glory of God' (God's own glory her light and splendor, the presence of God illumining the city, v.23, her radiance not her own but God's glory shining in her); 'her light was like unto a stone most precious... like a jasper stone, clear as crystal' (a brilliant, translucent, precious radiance, the figure of pure, dazzling, glorious light, the city transparent with the glory of God, radiant as a flawless jewel) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: having the glory of God: and her light was like unto a stone most precious, even like a jasper stone, clear as crystal
the city radiant with the glory of God
the divine presence illumining the holy city
radiant as a flawless jewel, transparent with God's glory

schools.summary: The wall and twelve gates: 'and had a wall great and high, and had twelve gates, and at the gates twelve angels, and names written thereon, which are the names of the twelve tribes of the children of Israel' — the city having a great, high wall (security and separation) with twelve gates, twelve angels at the gates, and the names of the twelve tribes of Israel written on the gates (the city secure, accessible by twelve gates, bearing the names of God's covenant people); 'a wall great and high' marking the city's security and the exclusion of all evil (v.27), and the 'twelve gates' marking its full accessibility (three on each side, v.13, the city both safe and open); 'twelve angels' (guardians) and 'the names of the twelve tribes of the children of Israel' (the gates bearing the tribes' names, as Ezekiel 48:31, the city connected with the covenant people of the Old Testament, the twelve tribes, with the twelve apostles, v.14, marking the unity of the redeemed of both covenants) — Barnes, Robertson, JFB.

themes: a wall great and high, and had twelve gates, and at the gates twelve angels
names written thereon, which are the names of the twelve tribes of the children of Israel
the city's wall and twelve gates of the tribes
the secure city bearing the names of the covenant people

schools.summary: The gates on the four sides: 'on the east three gates; on the north three gates; on the south three gates; and on the west three gates' — the twelve gates arranged three on each of the four sides (the symmetrical, complete arrangement, drawn from Ezekiel 48:31-34, the city open on every side, accessible from all directions); three gates on each side marking the perfect, symmetrical order of the city and its full accessibility from every quarter (the redeemed coming from all directions, Matthew 8:11; Luke 13:29, into the city); the gates on east, north, south, and west marking the city's openness to all the redeemed from every part of the earth (the comprehensive, universal access to the holy city, none of the saved shut out by distance or direction) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: on the east three gates; on the north three gates; on the south three gates; and on the west three gates
the twelve gates on the four sides
the city accessible on every side, open to the redeemed from all directions
the symmetrical openness of the holy city

schools.summary: The twelve foundations: 'and the wall of the city had twelve foundations, and in them the names of the twelve apostles of the Lamb' — the city's wall having twelve foundations, bearing the names of the twelve apostles of the Lamb (the church built on the foundation of the apostles, Ephesians 2:20, their names honored in the foundations of the holy city); 'twelve foundations' marking the apostolic foundation of the church (built on the apostles' teaching and testimony, Ephesians 2:20; Matthew 16:18, the foundations bearing their names honoring the apostles' place in the city of God); 'the names of the twelve apostles of the Lamb' named in the foundations, as the twelve tribes are on the gates (v.12) — the redeemed of both covenants (Israel's tribes and Christ's apostles) united in the one city, the New Jerusalem honoring both the old covenant people (gates) and the apostolic church (foundations) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: the wall of the city had twelve foundations, and in them the names of the twelve apostles of the Lamb
the twelve foundations of the apostles
the city built on the apostolic foundation
the redeemed of both covenants united in the one city

schools.summary: The golden measuring-reed: 'and he that talked with me had a golden reed to measure the city, and the gates thereof, and the wall thereof' — the angel having a golden measuring-reed to measure the city, its gates, and its wall (as Ezekiel 40:3; Zechariah 2:1, the measuring marking the perfect proportions and the completeness of the city); 'a golden reed to measure the city' (the measuring-rod, golden for its preciousness, used to measure the city, gates, and wall, to display its exact, perfect dimensions, the measuring revealing the city's symmetry and completeness); the measuring of the city (vv.16-17) showing its perfect, complete, and ordered form (nothing wanting or amiss, the golden reed revealing the divine perfection of the holy city's proportions) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: he that talked with me had a golden reed to measure the city, and the gates thereof, and the wall thereof
the angel with a golden reed to measure the city
the measuring revealing the city's perfect proportions
the divine completeness of the holy city

schools.summary: The city foursquare and cubic: 'and the city lieth foursquare, and the length is as large as the breadth: and he measured the city with the reed, twelve thousand furlongs. The length and the breadth and the height of it are equal' — the city a perfect square (foursquare), its length and breadth equal (the figure of perfect symmetry and order, as the Holy of Holies was a cube, the city's regular, perfect form); 'twelve thousand furlongs (stadia, about 1,400-1,500 miles)' an immense, symbolic dimension (the vast size, twelve thousand, the number of the tribes multiplied, marking the greatness and capacity of the city, large enough for all the redeemed); 'the length and the breadth and the height of it are equal' (the city a perfect cube — length, breadth, height equal — like the Holy of Holies, 1 Kings 6:20, the figure of perfect, complete holiness and symmetry, the whole city a sanctuary, a perfect cube of glory) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the city lieth foursquare, and the length is as large as the breadth: and he measured the city with the reed, twelve thousand furlongs
The length and the breadth and the height of it are equal
the city foursquare, vast, and equal in dimensions
a perfect cube of glory, like the Holy of Holies

schools.summary: The wall's measure: 'and he measured the wall thereof, an hundred and forty and four cubits, according to the measure of a man, that is, of the angel' — the wall measuring 144 cubits (twelve times twelve, the symbolic number of the tribes/apostles); the measuring 'according to the measure of a man, that is, of the angel' (the human standard of measure used by the angel); '144 cubits' (12 × 12) the symbolic number associated with the people of God (the twelve tribes and twelve apostles, the 144,000, 7:4; 14:1, the wall's measure echoing the number of the redeemed, whether the wall's height or thickness, the number significant); 'according to the measure of a man, that is, of the angel' (the angel measuring by the ordinary human standard, the cubit, the measure intelligible to men, the angel using the human unit of measurement) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: he measured the wall thereof, an hundred and forty and four cubits, according to the measure of a man, that is, of the angel
the measure of the city's wall
144 cubits, the symbolic number of the people of God
the wall's measure echoing the redeemed

schools.summary: The wall and city of precious materials: 'and the building of the wall of it was of jasper: and the city was pure gold, like unto clear glass' — the wall built of jasper (the precious, brilliant stone, v.11), and the city itself pure gold, transparent like clear glass (the figure of the city's surpassing preciousness, purity, and brilliance); 'the wall of it was of jasper' (the jasper being the stone of the city's glory, v.11, marking the wall's brilliant, precious beauty, the city enclosed in a wall of dazzling jewel); 'the city was pure gold, like unto clear glass' (the city pure gold yet transparent as clear glass, the figure of perfect purity and brilliance, gold so refined it is translucent, the surpassing, glorious purity of the holy city, beyond any earthly gold) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: the building of the wall of it was of jasper: and the city was pure gold, like unto clear glass
the wall of jasper and the city of pure gold
the city's surpassing preciousness, purity, and brilliance
transparent gold of perfect purity

schools.summary: The jeweled foundations: 'and the foundations of the wall of the city were garnished with all manner of precious stones. The first foundation was jasper; the second, sapphire; the third, a chalcedony; the fourth, an emerald' — the twelve foundations (v.14, the apostles) adorned with twelve kinds of precious stones (the first four jasper, sapphire, chalcedony, and emerald, the figure of the surpassing beauty and preciousness of the city's foundations); 'garnished with all manner of precious stones' (adorned with every kind of jewel, recalling the high priest's breastplate, Exodus 28:17-20, the apostolic foundations gleaming with precious stones, the figure of glory and worth); the named stones (jasper, sapphire, chalcedony, emerald, and the rest, v.20) marking each foundation with a distinct, precious jewel (the variety and brilliance of the gems displaying the manifold glory of the city's foundations, each apostolic foundation a precious stone) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the foundations of the wall of the city were garnished with all manner of precious stones
The first foundation was jasper; the second, sapphire; the third, a chalcedony; the fourth, an emerald
the jeweled foundations of the city
the apostolic foundations gleaming with the manifold glory of jewels

schools.summary: The remaining foundation-stones: 'the fifth, sardonyx; the sixth, sardius; the seventh, chrysolite; the eighth, beryl; the ninth, a topaz; the tenth, a chrysoprasus; the eleventh, a jacinth; the twelfth, an amethyst' — the list of the twelve foundation-stones completed (each of the twelve apostolic foundations a distinct precious stone, the twelve gems together displaying the full, manifold glory of the city's foundations); the twelve stones (jasper through amethyst, vv.19-20) varied precious gems, many corresponding to the stones of the high priest's breastplate (Exodus 28, the variety marking the rich, manifold beauty of the foundations, the apostolic church's glory displayed in twelve jewels); the twelve stones, one for each apostolic foundation, completing the adornment (the city's foundations a comprehensive display of precious beauty, the full glory of the apostolic foundation of the church set forth in the twelve gems) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: The fifth, sardonyx; the sixth, sardius... the twelfth, an amethyst
the remaining jeweled foundations
the twelve precious stones completing the city's foundations
the apostolic foundations in the full glory of twelve jewels

schools.summary: The pearl gates and golden street: 'and the twelve gates were twelve pearls; every several gate was of one pearl: and the street of the city was pure gold, as it were transparent glass' — each of the twelve gates a single pearl, and the city's street pure gold, transparent as glass (the figure of the surpassing, costly beauty of the city — gates of pearl, streets of transparent gold); 'every several gate was of one pearl' (a single, immense pearl, the pearl a costly, beautiful gem, Matthew 13:46, the gates of the city of surpassing preciousness, the entrance to the holy city through gates of pearl); 'the street of the city was pure gold, as it were transparent glass' (the city's street/broad way pure, transparent gold, v.18, the figure of the perfect purity and brilliance underfoot, the very streets of translucent gold, the surpassing glory of the city in every part) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the twelve gates were twelve pearls; every several gate was of one pearl: and the street of the city was pure gold, as it were transparent glass
the pearl gates and the golden street
the surpassing, costly beauty of the city in every part
gates of pearl, streets of transparent gold

schools.summary: God and the Lamb the temple: 'and I saw no temple therein: for the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are the temple of it' — no temple in the holy city (in pointed contrast to the earthly Jerusalem, whose glory was its temple, the New Jerusalem having none, for God and the Lamb are themselves its temple); 'no temple therein' marking the perfect, immediate presence of God (in the old order the temple the place of God's special presence and the means of approach, in the new God immediately present everywhere, no special structure needed, for all is holy); 'the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are the temple of it' (God and the Lamb themselves the temple, the redeemed worshiping and communing with God directly without mediating structure, the immediate, unhindered presence of God and Christ the city's sanctuary, the consummation of all that the temple foreshadowed) — Barnes, Robertson, Henry, Ellicott.

themes: I saw no temple therein: for the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are the temple of it
God and the Lamb are the temple of the city
the immediate, unhindered presence of God the sanctuary of the redeemed
the consummation of all that the temple foreshadowed

schools.summary: God and the Lamb the light: 'and the city had no need of the sun, neither of the moon, to shine in it: for the glory of God did lighten it, and the Lamb is the light thereof' — the holy city needing no sun or moon (the created lights superseded, in the new order no natural luminaries required, the city having a greater light); 'the glory of God did lighten it' (God's own glory, his shekinah, v.11, illumining the city, Isaiah 60:19-20, the divine glory the city's light, God's presence its sun); 'the Lamb is the light (lamp) thereof' (the Lamb, Christ, the lamp of the city, God's glory shining and the Lamb the light-bearer through whom it shines, Christ, the light of the world, John 8:12, the everlasting light of the New Jerusalem, the redeemed walking in the light of God and the Lamb) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the city had no need of the sun, neither of the moon, to shine in it
for the glory of God did lighten it, and the Lamb is the light thereof
the glory of God and the Lamb are the city's light
the redeemed walking forever in the light of God and Christ

schools.summary: The saved nations and kings: 'and the nations of them which are saved shall walk in the light of it: and the kings of the earth do bring their glory and honour into it' — the saved nations walking in the light of the city (Isaiah 60:3, the redeemed of all nations living and walking in the light of God and the Lamb, the universal scope of the city's blessing, embracing the saved from all peoples); 'the nations of them which are saved' (or simply 'the nations,' the redeemed from all nations, walking in the city's light, the holy city the home of the saved from every people, the universal gathering of the redeemed); 'the kings of the earth do bring their glory and honour into it' (the kings bringing their glory and honor into the city, Isaiah 60:3-11, the figure of all earthly glory laid at the feet of God in the holy city, the redeemed kings, in contrast to the kings who served Babylon, bringing their honor to the New Jerusalem, all glory rendered to God) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the nations of them which are saved shall walk in the light of it
the kings of the earth do bring their glory and honour into it
the nations and kings bringing their glory into the city
the redeemed of all peoples gathered, all earthly glory rendered to God

schools.summary: The gates never shut: 'and the gates of it shall not be shut at all by day: for there shall be no night there' — the city's gates never shut, for there is no night (the gates, never closed, marking the perfect security and the constant, free access to the city, in a world without enemies no gate need ever be shut); 'the gates of it shall not be shut at all' (unlike earthly cities, which shut their gates at night against danger, the holy city's gates standing always open, perfect safety and welcome, free, perpetual access to the city of God); 'for there shall be no night there' (no night in the holy city, v.23; 22:5, the perpetual day of God's light, no darkness, no time of closing, the figure of unending light, security, and joy, the gates needing never to close because the day never ends) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the gates of it shall not be shut at all by day: for there shall be no night there
the gates never shut and the absence of night
perfect safety, free access, and the unending day of God's light
the city ever open in endless day

schools.summary: The glory of the nations brought in: 'and they shall bring the glory and honour of the nations into it' — the glory and honor of the nations brought into the holy city (resuming v.24; Isaiah 60:5, 11, all that is excellent and honorable among the nations gathered into the city of God, the redeemed nations rendering their glory to God); 'the glory and honour of the nations' (the best, the noblest, the most excellent of the nations brought in, whatever is truly glorious and honorable consecrated to God in the holy city, the nations' glory finding its home and end in the New Jerusalem); the nations' glory 'brought into it' (laid up in the city of God, all true glory rendered to God and gathered into his city, the redeemed peoples consecrating their honor to God, the universal homage of the nations to the holy city) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: they shall bring the glory and honour of the nations into it
the glory of the nations brought into the city
all that is excellent among the nations consecrated to God
the universal homage to the New Jerusalem

schools.summary: Nothing unclean enters: 'and there shall in no wise enter into it any thing that defileth, neither whatsoever worketh abomination, or maketh a lie: but they which are written in the Lamb's book of life' — nothing unclean, no one who works abomination or falsehood, able to enter the holy city (the perfect purity of the New Jerusalem, all defilement, abomination, and lying wholly excluded); 'any thing that defileth, neither whatsoever worketh abomination, or maketh a lie' (every defiling, abominable, and false thing shut out, the city admitting nothing unholy, the contrast with Babylon, full of abomination, 17:4-5, the holy city wholly clean); 'but they which are written in the Lamb's book of life' (only the redeemed, whose names are in the book of life, 20:12, 15; 13:8, entering, the book of life the test of entrance, only the saved, the citizens of the city, may enter, the redeemed alone inhabiting the holy city) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: there shall in no wise enter into it any thing that defileth, neither whatsoever worketh abomination, or maketh a lie
but they which are written in the Lamb's book of life
nothing unclean enters, only those in the Lamb's book of life
the holy city wholly clean, inhabited by the redeemed alone
